codes.description:
The request could not be parsed. The artefact is not valid JSON, or
does not conform to the schema, or is missing required fields.
codes.disclosure_note: Safe to disclose — no cryptographic information leaked.

codes.description:
The artefact's `protocol_version` major does not match any version
supported by the consumer. The consumer MUST refuse before any
further processing.

The canonicalisation profile specified in the artefact is not
recognised, OR the canonicalisation process failed (e.g. the input
contains a float, exceeds the depth limit, or exceeds the size limit).

No proof was supplied with the request. The boundary requires a proof
for this action.

The proof was examined and found to be invalid. This is the
umbrella code that is disclosed to public callers. The specific
reason (signature invalid, action mismatch, etc.) is carried in
`internal_code` when the disclosure policy permits.
codes.disclosure_note:
This is the DEFAULT disclosed code for all proof-validity failures
under the `public` disclosure policy. It deliberately does NOT
distinguish signature failures from action mismatches, to avoid
leaking cryptographic diagnostics to untrusted callers.

codes.description:
The proof's issuer is not in the verifier's trust store. The
signature may be cryptographically valid but the issuer is not
authorised to issue proofs for this resource.
codes.disclosure_note:
Detailed code; only emitted under `trusted` disclosure. The public
disclosure collapses this to `PROOF_INVALID` to avoid revealing
which issuers the verifier trusts.

codes.description:
The proof's cryptographic signature does not verify under the
issuer's published public key. The proof is forged or was signed
with a different key.
codes.disclosure_note:
Detailed code; only emitted under `trusted` disclosure. Public
callers see `PROOF_INVALID` instead, to avoid leaking information
about the signature verification process.

codes.description: The proof's `expires_at` is in the past.
codes.disclosure_note: Safe to disclose — the expiry is in the proof itself.

codes.description:
The proof's `not_before` is in the future. The caller may retry
after `not_before`.
codes.disclosure_note: Safe to disclose — the not_before is in the proof itself.

codes.description:
The proof was minted for a different audience (verifier service)
than the one receiving it.
codes.disclosure_note:
Detailed code; only emitted under `trusted` disclosure. Public
callers see `PROOF_INVALID` to avoid leaking the verifier's identity.

codes.description:
The proof's `target` does not match the resource that the verifier
is protecting.
The proof's `action.type` does not match the action being attempted.

The proof's `action.parameters` do not match the parameters of the
action being attempted. The `action_hash` recomputed by the verifier
does not match the `action_hash` in the proof.

The proof has already been used. The replay store records a prior
execution under this `proof_id` with a different `exec_` identifier.

Note: a retry with the SAME `exec_` identifier is NOT a replay; it
is an idempotent retry and returns the prior outcome (if supported).

The authority under which the proof was minted has been revoked.
The proof may still be cryptographically valid, but the authority
broker has cancelled it (kill switch, parent grant revoked, etc.).

The authority broker's policy engine refused to authorise this
action, even though the proof may be cryptographically valid.
Examples: budget exceeded, scope denied, out of scope, rate limited.
codes.disclosure_note: Safe to disclose at this granularity.

codes.description:
The broker could not resolve a scoped provider credential for this
action. The credential may be missing, expired, or quarantined.

The protected provider refused to execute the action. The proof was
valid and the credential was available, but the provider returned
an explicit refusal (e.g. the provider's own policy engine denied
the request).

The protected provider failed to execute the action due to an
internal error (timeout, network, 5xx). The proof was valid but
the outcome is unknown.

The verifier cannot determine the outcome. This is the catch-all
for unexpected errors. It is also the code that consumers SHOULD
use when they encounter a refusal code they do not recognise
(forward compatibility).

disclosure_policies.name: public
disclosure_policies.description:
Default policy. Safe for untrusted callers. Emits the umbrella
`PROOF_INVALID` code for all proof-validity failures. Does not
populate `internal_code`.

disclosure_policies.name: trusted
disclosure_policies.description:
Policy for trusted callers (internal services, audit logs, debug
mode). Populates both `disclosed_code` and `internal_code`.

disclosure_policies.name: local_debug
disclosure_policies.description:
Policy for local development only. Populates both fields and may
include additional diagnostic context in the `message` field.
MUST NOT be used in production.
